options.B: trading cost for index-based portfolio managers is likely to be lower than trading cost for active managers who are suspected by their counterparties to possess an information advantage.
options.C: Their research finds no cost difference between index and active trading.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The finding runs the other way — counterparties fearing informed trading charge active managers more.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the reading: 'A secondary component of trading costs that contributes to tracking error is the trading commission paid to brokers. Commission costs make excess returns more negative and also affect tracking error. According to Perold and Salomon (1991), the trading cost for index-based portfolio managers is likely to be lower than the trading cost for active managers who are suspected by their counterparties to possess an information advantage.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. A difference exists: index managers' uninformed flow trades cheaper.

questions.stem: *Controlling tracking error* *most likely* involves trade-offs. Portfolio managers *most likely*:

options.A: ignore constraints.
options.B: hold much cash.
options.C: minimize cash held (creates undesirable TE).

rationales.A: Manage carefully.
rationales.B: Beta = 1 target.
rationales.C:
Full statement: minimize cash held (creates undesirable TE); invest cash flows at same valuations used by benchmark index provider (not always feasible); aim to maintain beta of 1.0 relative to benchmark index while keeping other risk factor exposures similar to index; net investor cash inflows/redemptions; use equitization tools.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The process of controlling tracking error involves trade-offs between the benefits and costs of maintaining complete faithfulness to the benchmark index... Portfolio managers who are unconstrained would keep the number of constituent securities and their weights as closely aligned to the benchmark index as possible. Even so, trading costs and other fees cause actual investment performance to deviate from index performance. Managers trade to accommodate inflows and outflows of cash from investors, to reinvest dividends, and to reflect changes in constituents of the underlying index. Most index-based portfolio managers attempt to minimize cash held because a cash position generally creates undesirable tracking error. To keep tracking error low, portfolio managers need to invest cash flows received at the same valuations used by the benchmark index provider. Of course, because this is not always feasible, portfolio managers aim to maintain a beta of 1.0 relative to the benchmark index, while keeping other risk factor exposures similar to those of the index.'

questions.stem: *Data frequency* affects tracking error measurement because *most likely*:

options.A: value will differ depending on whether data frequency is daily or less frequent.
options.B: no effect.
options.C: always identical.

rationales.A:
Full statement: value will differ depending on whether data frequency is daily or less frequent; for an index fund, degree of tracking error fluctuates over time.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For an index fund, the degree of tracking error fluctuates over time. Also, the value will differ depending on whether the data frequency is daily or less frequent.' Daily vs monthly vs annual measurement produces different TE values because return volatility scales with time period.
rationales.B: Frequency matters.
rationales.C: Frequency-dependent.

questions.stem: The knowledge check example (Australia funds' TE < 1/10 Korean funds' TE) *most likely* is *because*:

options.A: identical portfolios.
options.B: Australian funds have far more constituents than Korean funds and lower explicit fund fees.
options.C: no explanation.

rationales.A: Sampling vs replication.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Australian funds have far more constituents than Korean funds and lower explicit fund fees; Korean funds hold ~80% of index constituents (using sampling) while Australian funds hold more like ~100% (replication); Australian negative excess returns close to management fees of 18-20 bps.

Correct. Per the reading's knowledge check solution: 'Based on the number of stocks in the fund compared to the index constituent number, it appears most funds are attempting to replicate. Two of the funds (Macquarie True Index and KIM Cruise Index) have 80% to 90% of the stocks in the index, which indicates they are more likely to be using sampling. One fund (Samsung Index Premium) actually holds more than the index, which can happen if buffering is used. No fund contains the same number of stocks as constituents in the index. Thus, it is not surprising that the funds failed to track their respective indexes perfectly. On an annualized basis, tracking error for the Australian funds is less than one-tenth the level of the Korean funds. However, the Korean funds' excess return—which is fund return less the benchmark index return—is positive in all three cases. The negative excess returns for two of the Australian funds are relatively close and possibly attributable to their management fees of 18–20 basis points.'
rationales.C: Structural differences.

questions.stem: *Perfect zero tracking error* is *most likely* impossible because:

options.A: Perfect zero tracking error is routinely achievable with modern trading technology.
options.B: Tracking error has no mathematical property that prevents it from reaching zero.
options.C: even a manager whose return equals the index could have lagged then led producing net difference of zero — but being a standard deviation, tracking error cannot be zero in such cases.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Costs, timing differences, and cash frictions ensure some deviation always exists.
rationales.B: Incorrect. As a standard deviation of return differences, any lead-lag variation registers even when cumulative difference nets to zero.
rationales.C:
Full statement: even a manager whose return equals the index could have lagged then led producing net difference of zero — but being a standard deviation, tracking error cannot be zero in such cases; only truly perfect replication with zero timing differences produces zero TE.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In principle, a manager whose return is identical to that of the index could have arrived at that point by lagging and subsequently leading the index, producing a net difference of zero. But being a standard deviation, tracking error cannot be zero in cases such as the one described.' Any per-period deviations, even if they average to zero, contribute to non-zero TE.

drills.los_text: explain sources of return and risk to an index-based equity portfolio

questions.stem: The *sources of return* for an equity index replication portfolio *most likely* include:

options.A: company-specific returns, sector returns, country returns, and currency returns — same as for any actively managed fund.
options.B: only market return.
options.C: no attribution possible.

rationales.A:
Full statement: company-specific returns, sector returns, country returns, and currency returns — same as for any actively managed fund; can also group by portfolio objective (e.g., high dividend yield indexed portfolio grouped by dividend yield vs broad market benchmark; low volatility portfolio grouped by volatility buckets).

Correct. Per the reading: 'The sources of return for an equity index replication portfolio are the same as for any actively managed fund and include company-specific returns, sector returns, country returns, and currency returns. Beyond the traditional methods of grouping the risk and returns of the indexed portfolio, portfolio managers can group their indexed portfolios according to the stated portfolio objective. For example, a high dividend yield indexed portfolio may be grouped against the broad market benchmark by dividend yield. A low volatility portfolio could be grouped by volatility buckets.'
rationales.B: Multiple sources.
rationales.C: Attribution possible.

questions.stem: *Portfolio attribution analysis* for an index fund *most likely*:

options.A: irrelevant for index.
options.B: helps understand sources of return.
options.C: no analysis needed.

rationales.A: Relevant even for index.
rationales.B:
Full statement: helps understand sources of return; index fund managers who track a broad market index need to understand what factors are driving returns of portfolio and underlying index; factor-based strategy managers should understand sources of return AND how those returns relate to broad market from which constituents were chosen — similar to actively managed funds since factor-based strategies are actively chosen.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Most portfolio managers will rely on their portfolio attribution system to help them in understanding the sources of return. Index fund managers who track a broad market index need to understand what factors are driving the returns of that portfolio and its underlying index. Index fund managers of factor-based strategies should understand both the sources of return for their indexed portfolios and how those returns relate to the broad market index from which the constituents were chosen. In this way, factor-based strategies are similar to actively managed funds in the sense that they are actively chosen.'
rationales.C: Valuable analysis.

questions.stem: In *Exhibit 8 attribution analysis example*, Portfolio X return of 5.62% vs benchmark 5.65% *most likely* indicates:

options.A: poor tracking.
options.B: large deviation.
options.C: close replication — only 3 basis points of return difference; portfolio is undoubtedly tracking index closely.

rationales.A: Very tight.
rationales.B: 3 bps = tight.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading's Exhibit 8 section: 'Portfolio X is an index fund that seeks to replicate the performance of its benchmark. The manager of Portfolio X confirms that the portfolio, which has a return of 5.62%, is closely replicating the performance of the benchmark, which has a return of 5.65%. Using Exhibit 8, the manager analyzes the relative sector weights and sources of the three basis points of return difference. A portfolio that is within three basis points of its benchmark index is undoubtedly tracking the index closely.'

questions.stem: *Securities lending* by index fund managers *most likely* provides:

options.A: additional revenue offset against portfolio management costs — often resulting in lower net fees to investors.
options.B: no revenue.
options.C: always cost.

rationales.A:
Full statement: additional revenue offset against portfolio management costs — often resulting in lower net fees to investors; example: iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) $63.9B AUM lent $5.97B in securities as of 31 March 2021 (100% collateralized with cash); net securities lending income ~$63M nearly offset ~$90.7M in investment advisory fees.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A popular exchange-traded fund (ETF) represents a good example of how securities lending revenue can provide a benefit to investment beneficiaries. As of 31 March 2021, the USD63.9 billion iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) had lent USD5.97 billion in securities to various counterparties. This amount was 100% collateralized with cash. An affiliated party, BlackRock Institutional Trust Company, served as the securities lending agent in exchange for 4 basis points of collateral investment fees annually. IWM's net securities lending income for the year was slightly above USD63 million, which nearly offset the approximately USD90.7 million in investment advisory fees charged by the portfolio managers.'
rationales.B: Revenue source.
rationales.C: Substantial revenue.

questions.stem: *Global securities lending market* size (June 30 2021) per ISLA *most likely* was:

options.A: small market.
options.B: EUR 28 trillion in securities made available for lending by institutional investors.
options.C: no lending.

rationales.A: Substantial market.
rationales.B:
Full statement: EUR 28 trillion in securities made available for lending by institutional investors; of this EUR 2.6 trillion in value was actually loaned; collective investment vehicles and pension funds accounted for 59% of total value of securities loaned; collateral held with European triparty agents: equities 45%, government bonds 44%.

Correct. Per the reading: 'According to the International Securities Lending Association (2021), the 30 June 2021 global value of securities made available for lending by institutional investors was EUR28 trillion. Of this, EUR2.6 trillion in value was actually loaned. Collective investment vehicles and pension funds accounted for 59% of the total value of securities loaned. Collateral held with European triparty agents was in line with previous historical norms with equities and government bonds representing 45% and 44% of reported collateral, respectively.'
rationales.C: €28T/€2.6T loaned.

questions.stem: *Securities lending risks* *most likely* include:

options.A: no risk.
options.B: always low risk.
options.C: credit risk (borrower credit quality), market risk (value of posted collateral), liquidity risk, operational risk.

rationales.A: Real risks.
rationales.B: Multiple risks.
rationales.C:
Full statement: credit risk (borrower credit quality), market risk (value of posted collateral), liquidity risk, operational risk; collateral investment risk (if lender invests cash collateral in long-term/risky securities, collateral value at risk of erosion); Sigma Finance Company 2008-2009 example: SIV downgraded, lost short-term borrowing ability, defaulted; investors who lent securities suffered substantial losses.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Securities lending carries risks that can offset the benefits. The main risks are the credit quality of the borrower (credit risk) and the value of the posted collateral (market risk), although liquidity risk and operational risk are additional considerations. Lenders are permitted to sell loaned securities at any time under the normal course of the portfolio management mandate, and the borrowed shares must be returned in time for normal settlement of that sale. However, there is no guarantee that the borrower can deliver on a timely basis. An additional risk is that lenders can invest cash held as collateral; and if a lender elects to invest the cash in long-term or risky securities, the collateral value is at risk of erosion... The example of Sigma Finance Company illustrates collateral investment risk. Sigma Finance was a structured investment vehicle... During the credit 2008–2009 global financial crisis, Sigma was downgraded by the rating agencies and lost its ability to borrow in the short-term markets, which led to default. Investors in Sigma's credit offerings, many of them security lenders, suffered substantial losses because of the default.'

questions.stem: *Institutional investors* like index mutual funds and pension funds are *most likely*:

options.A: viewed as preferred lenders because they are long-term holders of shares and unlikely to claim their shares back abruptly from borrowers (Duffie, Gârleanu, and Pedersen 2002).
options.B: Index funds and pension funds are avoided as securities lenders because their holdings turn over rapidly.
options.C: Institutional index investors are considered unreliable lenders prone to abrupt recalls.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'According to Duffie, Gârleanu, and Pedersen (2002), institutional investors such as index mutual funds and pension funds are viewed as preferred lenders because they are long-term holders of shares and unlikely to claim their shares back abruptly from borrowers.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Their holdings are notably STABLE — that stability is why borrowers prefer them.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Long-term, low-turnover holders are the least likely to recall shares abruptly, making them preferred, not unreliable.

questions.stem: *Borrowing cost* for securities lending *most likely* is:

options.A: Securities lending generates no fees because shares are lent as a market courtesy.
options.B: typically 2-10% annualized cost of borrowing depending on borrower's credit quality and how difficult the security is to borrow.
options.C: Borrowing fees are fixed at 25% annualized for all securities regardless of borrowing difficulty.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Lending is a fee business — typically 2%–10% annualized.
rationales.B:
Full statement: typically 2-10% annualized cost of borrowing depending on borrower's credit quality and how difficult the security is to borrow; some securities are widely recognized as 'easy to borrow' (ETB); borrowers pay cash to lenders in lieu of any cash dividends received.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Borrowers take formal legal title to the securities, receive all cash flows and voting rights, and pay an annualized cost of borrowing (typically 2–10%). The borrowing cost depends on the borrower's credit quality and how difficult it is to borrow the security in question. Some securities are widely recognized as "easy to borrow" (ETB).' Also: 'Similarly, borrowers must pay cash to lenders in lieu of any cash dividends received.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Fees vary with borrower credit and how hard the security is to borrow; no fixed 25% exists.

questions.stem: *Investor activism* by index-based fund managers *most likely* addresses:

options.A: Index-based fund managers cannot engage in investor activism because they never choose their holdings.
options.B: Indexer engagement is limited to attending annual meetings without voting.
options.C: governance policies, executive compensation, and social, environmental, and strategy issues — via voting shares (large influence in corporate elections) and access to private meetings with corporate management.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Holding choice is irrelevant to activism; large index managers wield major proxy-voting influence.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Voting shares is the core mechanism, applied to governance, compensation, and social/environmental/strategy issues.
rationales.C:
Full statement: governance policies, executive compensation, and social, environmental, and strategy issues — via voting shares (large influence in corporate elections) and access to private meetings with corporate management; typical points of contact: investor relations specialists, general counsel/corporate secretary, board chair, CEO/CFO.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Institutional investors, especially index fund managers, are among the largest shareholders of many companies. The shares that they vote can have a large influence on corporate elections and outcomes of the proxy process. Their status as large shareholders often gives such investors access to private meetings with corporate management to discuss their concerns and preferences regarding corporate policies on board structure and composition, management compensation, operational risk management, the integrity of accounting statements, and other matters. Goldstein (2014) reports that in a survey, about two-thirds of public companies indicate investor engagement in 2014 was higher than it had been three years earlier. The typical points of contact were investor relations specialists, general counsel/corporate secretary, the board chair, and the CEO or CFO of the company. The respondents also reported that engagement is now covering more topics, but the subject matter is not principally financial. Governance policies, executive compensation, and social, environmental, and strategy issues are dominant.'

questions.stem: The *rationale for governance activism* by index-based managers *most likely* is:

options.A: governance improvements aimed at improving effectiveness of operations, management, and board oversight — if resulting efficiency improvements evidenced in higher returns to index-constituent stocks, index performance rises and so does the performance of an index-tracking portfolio.
options.B: Governance is irrelevant to index-based managers because they cannot sell poorly governed companies.
options.C: Index managers pursue governance activism purely for regulatory credit, with no return rationale.

rationales.A:
Full statement: governance improvements aimed at improving effectiveness of operations, management, and board oversight — if resulting efficiency improvements evidenced in higher returns to index-constituent stocks, index performance rises and so does the performance of an index-tracking portfolio; goal is to increase returns; may have higher duty than transient active managers since as long as stock is in benchmark index, index-based managers are permanent shareholders.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Why should governance matter for index-based investors in broadly diversified portfolios? Across such portfolios, governance quality is broadly diversified; moreover, by definition, index-based investors do not try to select the best-performing companies or avoid the worst. However, corporate governance improvements are aimed at improving the effectiveness of the operations, management, and board oversight of the business. If the resulting efficiency improvements are evidenced in higher returns to index-constituent stocks, the index performance rises and so does the performance of an index-tracking portfolio. Thus, a goal of activism is to increase returns. Index-based investors may even have a higher duty than more-transient active managers to use their influence to improve governance. As long as a stock has membership in the benchmark index, index-based managers can be considered permanent shareholders.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Inability to sell is precisely why engagement matters — improvement is the indexer's only lever, and efficiency gains flow to fund returns.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The rationale is return-enhancing: better operations, management, and oversight lift the value of held shares.

questions.stem: *Costs of activist engagement* *most likely* include:

options.A: no costs.
options.B: staff resources to become familiar with key issues and engage management, regulators, and other investors.
options.C: always free.

rationales.A: Real costs.
rationales.B:
Full statement: staff resources to become familiar with key issues and engage management, regulators, and other investors; researching and voting thousands of proxy ballots (many managers hire proxy voting service like Institutional Shareholder Services or Broadridge Financial Services); potential conflicts of interest (e.g., financial firm managing retirement plan of company whose stock is index constituent may hesitate to challenge that company's management).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Although shareholder return can be enhanced by engagement, the costs of these measures must also be considered. Among the more significant costs are staff resources required to become familiar with key issues and to engage management, regulators, and other investors. Researching and voting thousands of proxy ballots becomes problematic for many managers. They frequently hire a proxy voting service, such as Institutional Shareholder Services or Broadridge Financial Services, to achieve their goal of voting the proxy ballots in their clients' favor. Although a strong argument can be made in favor of even index-based managers voting their shares in an informed way and pursuing governance changes when warranted, potential conflicts of interest may limit investors' propensity to challenge company management.'
rationales.C: Substantial costs.

questions.stem: *Time-phased voting rights* *most likely*:

options.A: Time-phased voting rights penalize long-term shareholders by diluting their votes over time.
options.B: No company has ever implemented time-phased voting rights.
options.C: some companies give greater voting rights to long-term shareholders (Dallas and Barry 2016) — 12 US companies with voting rights that increase to four, five, or even ten votes per share if holding period exceeds three (and sometimes four) years.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The structure REWARDS tenure — voting power increases with holding period.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Documented US examples exist (a dozen companies per Dallas and Barry 2016).
rationales.C:
Full statement: some companies give greater voting rights to long-term shareholders (Dallas and Barry 2016) — 12 US companies with voting rights that increase to four, five, or even ten votes per share if holding period exceeds three (and sometimes four) years; reinforces permanence of index-based holders.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Reinforcing the concept of permanence, some companies even give greater voting rights to long-term shareholders. Dallas and Barry (2016) examine 12 US companies with voting rights that increase to four, five, or even ten votes per share if the holding period is greater than three and sometimes four years.'
